column2: Breakdown of U.S.–Iran negotiations increasing risk of military conflict
column9: No formal OPEC response; Saudi Arabia quietly raised output by ~500k bpd ahead of expected conflict
column10: Rising geopolitical uncertainty added risk premium due to potential future supply disruptions
column11: Brent climbed $1.70 on the day as Trump's public rebuke of Iran negotiators signalled imminent military action. Markets had been pricing a 40% probability of U.S.-Israel strikes within two weeks; the statement pushed that expectation above 70%. Risk premium drove the bulk of the move. Physical supply remained intact but forward markets tightened sharply.
column12: Trump: "I'm not happy with the way Iran is negotiating."

column2: Suspension of oil shipments through Strait of Hormuz following Iran conflict escalation
column9: OPEC+ emergency meeting: agreed +206k bpd for April — widely seen as insufficient vs. 12 mb/d loss
column10: Immediate disruption to a major global supply route reduced available oil flows
column11: U.S.–Israeli strikes on Iranian military sites overnight triggered Iran's IRGC to declare the Strait of Hormuz closed to navigation. ~20% of global daily oil supply (?20 mb/d) transits the strait. Within hours, hundreds of tankers anchored outside the waterway. OPEC+ convened an emergency call, agreeing to a token +206k bpd increase — markets saw this as confirming spare capacity is near zero. Brent surged $7.33 on the single largest one-day supply shock since the 1990 Gulf War.
column12: Iran Revolutionary Guards: "No ship is allowed to pass the Strait of Hormuz."

column2: U.S.–Israel strikes on Iran trigger supply disruptions and shipping risks
column9: U.S. Treasury issued 30-day waiver on sanctioned Russian crude already en route (~100 mb total)
column10: Escalating conflict raised fears of prolonged supply shortages and restricted oil flows
column11: Second consecutive day of massive upward pressure. Overnight airstrikes hit Iranian radar and air-defence sites, while IRGC drones attacked two tankers in the Gulf of Oman. The U.S. Treasury's emergency 30-day sanction waiver on Russian crude already at sea was announced to soften the blow, but markets interpreted this as confirmation that the disruption was serious enough to require emergency action. The cumulative +14% two-day rally was the largest since the 1973 OPEC embargo.
column12: Iran Revolutionary Guards: "We will set fire to any ship attempting to transit the Strait of Hormuz."

column2: Closure of Strait of Hormuz and widespread Middle East production shutdowns
column7: Saudi Aramco / Kuwait Petroleum / UAE ADNOC
column9: Saudi Arabia reportedly raised its own output 500k bpd; UAE rerouting via Abu Dhabi–Fujairah bypass pipeline
column10: Severe supply disruption from halted exports and reduced output tightened global oil availability
column11: With tanker traffic effectively nil through Hormuz, Gulf producers began shutting wells as storage filled. Kuwait and Saudi Arabia reported up to 15 mb/d of effective shut-in or stranded supply — equivalent to 15% of world demand. The UAE began rerouting crude via the 1.5 mb/d Abu Dhabi–Fujairah pipeline, bypassing the strait, but this covers only a fraction of normal flows. Trump floated the idea of U.S. Navy escorts, but the Pentagon warned this would require weeks to organise.
column12: Trump: "The U.S. Navy could begin escorting oil tankers through the Strait of Hormuz."

column2: Ongoing Strait of Hormuz supply disruptions and rising inventory draw expectations
column9: No new policy intervention; Goldman Sachs issued note warning of $110–$150 range if disruption lasts >21 days
column10: Anticipation of supply shortages and declining inventories supported elevated price levels
column11: Flat close on high volumes masked extreme intraday volatility (intraday range >$6). The market was digesting Goldman Sachs' note that 21+ days of disruption would exhaust global strategic reserve buffers. OECD inventories were already at 5-year lows heading into the crisis. The day's stasis reflected uncertainty: buyers feared missing a continued rally; sellers feared capitulating into an already-priced-in shock. Tanker queue outside Hormuz grew to ~400 vessels.
column12: Goldman Sachs: "Oil flows through Hormuz remain severely disrupted, with major supply losses expected."

column2: Escalation of Middle East conflict disrupting Strait of Hormuz tanker traffic
column7: Iran IRGC / Lloyd's of London / Windward AI
column9: Lloyd's war risk surcharge on Hormuz-adjacent routes raised to 2.5% of vessel value per voyage
column10: Rising geopolitical risk and reduced oil transport capacity tightened global supply expectations
column11: Fresh Iranian drone attacks on two Greek-flagged tankers outside the strait. Lloyd's of London raised war risk surcharges to 2.5% of hull value per transit — effectively making Hormuz insurance prohibitively expensive for most operators. Windward AI data showed only 21 tankers had moved through the strait since 28 Feb vs. a pre-conflict daily average of 20+ vessels. The insurance squeeze accelerated the physical withdrawal of shipping capacity and pushed freight rates on alternative Cape of Good Hope routes to record levels.
column12: Market strategist: "Ongoing conflict in Hormuz is disrupting oil flows and raising inflation concerns."

column2: Severe disruption of Strait of Hormuz flows with no immediate policy intervention
column6: Supply Shock / Demand for SPR Release
column9: IEA held emergency consultations; formal SPR release vote pending — no decision yet
column10: Sharp supply shock and lack of immediate government action increased fears of prolonged supply shortages
column11: The largest single-day gain since the crisis began. News that IEA members were debating but had not yet authorised a reserve release fuelled the squeeze — the market's message was 'act now.' Trump threatened Iran with 'imminent action' and the Pentagon moved two additional carrier strike groups to the region. Qatar announced force majeure on Ras Laffan LNG exports (adding gas supply fears on top of oil). S&P Global estimated global supply loss at ~8 mb/d net after partial bypass flows. Brent closed at $92.69.
column12: Trump: "We demand Iran's unconditional surrender and may take imminent action in Hormuz."

column2: Ongoing Middle East conflict with no significant change in supply conditions
column9: White House signals openness to 'humanitarian corridor' discussions; no formal progress
column10: Market stabilisation as no new supply disruptions or escalations altered existing expectations
column11: Iran's President offered a conditional halt to attacks on neighbouring Gulf states — 'unless attacked' — which markets read as a very thin de-escalation signal. No tankers transited. The flat close reflected a market that had priced in sustained disruption and was now watching for the next catalyst. Short-sellers covered partially, but fresh longs were reluctant at $93/bbl without confirmation of supply restoration. WTI–Brent spread widened to $14 as U.S. landlocked crude held closer to domestic supply.
column12: Iran President: "We will halt attacks on neighboring countries unless attacked."

column2: First tanker successfully transits Strait of Hormuz under informal Chinese negotiation
column9: China confirmed back-channel negotiations with Iran for safe passage of Chinese-associated vessels
column10: Selective Iranian safe-passage permits created temporary relief and profit-taking
column11: The Shenlong (Liberia-flagged Suezmax, managed by Greek operator Dynacom) transited the strait carrying ~1 mb of Saudi crude bound for Mumbai — the first non-Iranian cargo through since 28 Feb. Iran appeared to be granting informal safe passage to vessels with Chinese interests or non-U.S.-allied cargoes. Markets took this as a partial de-escalation signal. However, Lloyd's Intelligence noted only 21 tankers total had moved in 10 days vs. 200+ normal. The dip was a profit-taking correction, not a structural reversal.
column12: S&P Global: "Selective safe passages suggest Iran is using Hormuz access as a geopolitical bargaining chip."

column2: Iran escalates attacks on Gulf energy infrastructure despite selective tanker passages
column7: Iran IRGC / UAE ADNOC / Saudi Aramco
column9: UAE declared temporary airspace closure; Saudi Arabia intercepted 'dozens of drones' over Aramco facilities
column10: New attacks on Gulf energy infrastructure undermined earlier relief and reignited supply fears
column11: Despite the previous day's tanker transit, Iran launched drone and missile salvos at UAE energy terminals (Fujairah oil hub struck, loading operations suspended) and Saudi Aramco facilities near Abqaiq. UAE declared a temporary national airspace closure. The combination of infrastructure attacks and continued Hormuz chokepoint constraints drove Brent back above $94. The market re-priced the risk that even 'bypass' routes and terminals were now vulnerable, eliminating the safety valve premium that had kept prices below $95.
column12: UAE Defense Ministry: "Air defense systems actively intercepting missile and drone threats throughout the morning."

column2: IEA emergency consultations on record strategic petroleum reserve (SPR) release
column7: IEA / G7 Energy Ministers / U.S. DOE
column9: IEA members begin voting on 400 mb emergency release — largest in IEA history; U.S. to contribute 172 mb
column10: Anticipation of strategic reserve release provided temporary relief to market sentiment
column11: Ahead of the formal IEA decision the following day, energy ministers from G7 nations signalled unanimous support for a record SPR release. Reports of 400 mb total — vs. the 182 mb deployed after Russia's 2022 Ukraine invasion — briefly pulled Brent below $93. Analysts cautioned that the release would be spread over 120 days (?3.3 mb/d), a fraction of the 20 mb/d normally transiting Hormuz. Goldman Sachs maintained its $110–$150 price band for scenarios of continued closure beyond 30 days.
column12: IEA Director: "The conflict is creating the most extensive disruption in history of global oil markets."

column2: IEA authorises record 400 million-barrel SPR release; Iran escalates strikes
column9: IEA: 400 mb release across 32 member nations; U.S. SPR 172 mb over 120 days; Japan to start releasing next week
column10: SPR release absorbed as insufficient vs. 20 mb/d Hormuz disruption; fresh Iranian strikes overshadowed relief
column11: Despite the largest-ever coordinated SPR release, Brent surged nearly $5.26 on the day as Iran simultaneously escalated strikes on Gulf infrastructure and ceasefire talks collapsed. Markets calculated that the IEA release delivers only ~3.3 mb/d — covering 16% of the ~20 mb/d Hormuz flow — and with delivery timelines of weeks to months before reaching Asian refineries. Chatham House analyst Neil Quilliam called the release 'a temporary fix.' The net effect: bullish physical reality overwhelmed the bearish policy signal.
column12: Al Jazeera: "The IEA release will not substantially alter the current crisis — it is a temporary fix."

column2: Reuters: World faces largest-ever oil supply disruption — IEA reports 8 mb/d loss in March
column9: IEA March Market Report: Gulf producers cut ?10 mb/d; global supply drop 8 mb/d net after partial bypass flows
column10: Quantified supply loss confirmed market's worst fears and sustained upward price momentum past $100
column11: Brent crossed $100/bbl for the first time since 2022 as the IEA's formal monthly market report quantified the damage: 8 mb/d net supply loss in March, with Middle East Gulf countries cutting ?10 mb/d (?10% of world demand). Wood Mackenzie warned that at 15 mb/d of Gulf supply gone, oil prices would need to hit $150 for demand destruction to rebalance the market. The psychological $100 barrier being crossed triggered algorithmic stop-losses and discretionary long-covering reversals across energy equity indices.
column12: Reuters: "Global supply to drop by 8 million bpd in March due to Strait of Hormuz blockade."

column2: IEA confirms worst-ever oil disruption; Goldman Sachs raises price forecasts to $110–$150
column9: Goldman Sachs raised Q4 2026 Brent forecast to $71/bbl base, $76/bbl upside; Citi flagged $120–$150 near-term
column10: Escalating analyst forecasts and no ceasefire prospects sustained buying pressure
column11: Goldman Sachs reset its oil price trajectory, assuming 21 days of near-zero Hormuz flows followed by 30 days of gradual recovery, implying $98/bbl average for March–April. In an upside scenario (30+ days disruption) it forecasted $110/bbl before retreating to $76/bbl Q4. Citi raised its 1–3 month Brent target to $120/bbl with a $150 bull case. Alpine Macro's chief geopolitical strategist warned 'peak war panic is 1–3 weeks away.' Prices tracked analyst revisions higher throughout the session.
column12: Fortune / Alpine Macro: "Peak war panic will likely hit markets in 1-3 weeks. The end is not in sight."

column2: Tanker attack near Strait of Hormuz; UAE airspace temporarily closed again
column9: UAE air defense systems 'actively intercepting' threats; loading at Fujairah terminal suspended again
column10: Profit-taking after six-day rally and limited fresh escalation led to modest pullback
column11: A tanker moored off Fujairah (UAE) was struck by a projectile — the first such incident in five days. UAE air defense intercepted multiple missiles and drones; a Pakistani national was killed by falling debris in Abu Dhabi from an interception. Despite the attack, oil pulled back as traders took profits after the 6-day, +15% rally. S&P Global counted only 21 total tanker transits in 16 days vs. pre-conflict norms of 100+/day. The modest dip reflected position-squaring rather than any genuine supply improvement.
column12: NYT: "A tanker moored off the UAE coast near the Strait of Hormuz was hit by a projectile."

column2: Ongoing Hormuz blockage with talks to secure shipping routes
column7: Trump / India Foreign Minister Jaishankar / Turkey
column9: India confirmed 'fruitful' direct talks with Iran; two Indian LPG vessels allowed safe passage
column10: Tentative diplomatic signals and some selective vessel passages eased pressure marginally
column11: India's Foreign Minister Jaishankar confirmed direct talks with Tehran were 'yielding results.' Two Shipping Corporation of India LPG tankers were granted safe passage. Turkey reported one of its vessels permitted transit after visiting an Iranian port (14 more Turkish ships still awaiting). These selective passages reinforced the 'informal filter' thesis — Iran targeting U.S.-allied cargoes while allowing neutral nations through. The near-flat close near $100 reflected a market in equilibrium between persistent supply fear and diplomatic noise.
column12: India FM Jaishankar: "I am currently engaged in conversations with Tehran and my discussions have some results."

column2: Partial easing in supply concerns as some ships crossed Strait of Hormuz
column9: U.S. Treasury Sec. Bessent: U.S. may lift sanctions on ~140 mb of Iranian crude currently on tankers
column10: Temporary market relief and profit-taking after strong rally as diplomatic signals multiplied
column11: Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent told Fox Business that the U.S. may lift sanctions on ~140 million barrels of Iranian crude sitting on tankers at sea 'in the upcoming days' — potentially reintroducing a significant volume to global supply. Israeli PM Netanyahu signalled support for reopening Hormuz and suggested Iran could no longer enrich uranium, hinting at possible endgame conditions. Brent fell 2.8% on the combination of diplomatic signals and profit-taking after the prior-week surge. Physical supply remained severely constrained.
column12: Bessent: "In the upcoming days, we may lift sanctions on Iranian oil currently on the water — about 140 million barrels."

column2: Ongoing Middle East conflict with tanker attack and continued closure of Strait of Hormuz
column9: White House confirmed 'tankers starting to dribble through' but admitted no material improvement
column10: Sustained supply constraints and geopolitical tension drove continued upward price pressure
column11: A Fujairah-anchored tanker was struck for a second time in three days. The White House press secretary used the phrase 'tankers starting to dribble through' — implying marginal, not meaningful, flow restoration. Reuters reported Iranian oil production and exports were continuing 'without interruption' for Iran's own cargoes to China, while blocking third-country flows. Trump's statement that he 'no longer needs NATO allies' assistance' introduced a new diplomatic uncertainty, raising fears of an isolated U.S. escalation that could shut flows entirely.
column12: Trump: "We no longer need, or desire, the NATO Countries' assistance."

column2: Attack on Iran–Qatar South Pars gas field escalates regional conflict
column7: Iran IRGC / Qatar / Fed Chair Powell
column9: Qatar Force Majeure on Ras Laffan LNG persists; Fed Chair Powell flags inflation risk from energy prices
column10: Supply disruption fears and closure of Strait of Hormuz increased global oil supply risk
column11: Strikes hit the shared Iran–Qatar South Pars/North Field gas complex — the world's single largest hydrocarbon reservoir. Qatar's QatarEnergy extended its Force Majeure declaration on Ras Laffan LNG exports. European gas futures surged 18% on the session. Fed Chair Powell's comment 'we will have to wait and see what happens in the Middle East' was interpreted as the Fed acknowledging stagflationary risks from the energy shock. CNBC confirmed 22 tankers carrying crude, LPG, and LNG remained inside the strait awaiting passage authorisation.
column12: Fed Chair Powell: "We will have to wait and see what happens in the Middle East."

column2: Escalation targeting major energy infrastructure including gas fields; Kuwait Mina al-Ahmadi refinery struck
column7: Iran IRGC / Kuwait National Petroleum / Saudi Arabia
column9: Kuwait KNPC: precautionary shutdown of Mina al-Ahmadi refinery (730k bpd capacity); Saudi Arabia intercepted 'dozens of drones'
column10: Increased risk of widespread supply disruption and prolonged shortages tightened market supply expectations
column11: Iranian drones struck Kuwait's Mina al-Ahmadi refinery (730k bpd capacity) and the adjacent Mina Abdullah refinery — the largest Kuwaiti refining complex. Saudi Arabia reported intercepting 'dozens' of drones over Aramco facilities. With downstream infrastructure now being targeted in addition to shipping, markets feared a permanent reduction in regional refining capacity. The $8.14 single-day gain (+7.58%) was the second-largest of the crisis. Iraq's Basra oil output fell to ~900k bpd from a pre-crisis 3.3 mb/d as storage capacity filled.
column12: Iran IRGC: "Energy facilities in the region are potential targets."

column2: Iraq declares force majeure on all foreign-operated oilfields; Kuwaiti refinery struck again; Trump rejects ceasefire
column7: Iraq Oil Ministry / SOMO / Reuters / Trump
column9: Iraq FM on all foreign-operated fields; Basra Oil Co output cut from 3.3 mb/d to ~900k bpd; no ceasefire
column10: Market pricing actual supply destruction as Iraq's 3+ mb/d loss is formalised alongside Kuwait refinery damage
column11: Iraq formally declared force majeure on all oilfields operated by foreign companies — BP, ExxonMobil, Shell, TotalEnergies, PetroChina all affected. Iraq's oil ministry ordered production cuts as storage capacity hit limits; SOMO (State Oil Marketing Organisation) unable to nominate tankers. Basra Oil Company output collapsed to ~900k bpd from 3.3 mb/d. Kuwait's Mina al-Ahmadi refinery was struck for a second consecutive day. Trump rejected a ceasefire and told reporters he was 'confident the Strait of Hormuz would open itself.' 3,400 additional Marines deployed to the region. Intraday high reached $118.
column12: Reuters: "Iraq has declared force majeure on oilfields operated by foreign companies due to Hormuz disruptions."

column2: Goldman Sachs resets oil price bets upward; Kuwait refinery targeted third consecutive day
column7: Goldman Sachs / Kuwait KPC / Arab News
column9: Goldman Sachs raised near-term Brent target; Iraq force majeure coverage expanded; no diplomatic breakthrough
column10: Continued infrastructure damage and rising analyst targets sustained elevated prices despite thin gains
column11: Goldman Sachs formally revised its war-scenario oil price model upward, citing the cumulative infrastructure damage in Kuwait, Iraq, and the UAE. Kuwait's Mina al-Ahmadi refinery was struck for a third consecutive day — KPC confirmed a fire in multiple units requiring precautionary partial shutdown. Saudi Arabia and the UAE also reported fresh drone interceptions. Iraq's force majeure coverage was confirmed to extend to all major international operators. With no diplomatic signal from Trump or Iran, prices held just above $112 on light Friday volumes. Bahrain reported an Iranian-linked drone ignited a warehouse fire.
column12: Arab News: "Drone attacks caused a fire at Kuwait's largest oil refinery — the facility targeted for a second day in a row."

column2: Oil whipsaws as U.S. and Iran threaten energy facilities; Bessent signals possible Iranian crude sanction lift
column9: Bessent reiterated 140 mb Iranian crude sanction-lift possibility; Netanyahu signalled conflict 'could conclude sooner'
column10: Competing signals on ceasefire prospects and sanction relief created sharp intraday swings and net selloff
column11: Monday session whipsawed violently — Brent traded from $114 intraday high to $105 low before settling at $107. Reuters reported U.S. and Iran were exchanging threats over energy facilities while simultaneously signalling openness to talks. Bessent reiterated the potential 140 mb Iranian crude release; Netanyahu said Iran 'no longer possesses the ability to enrich uranium,' a statement read as hinting at war aims being met. CNN reported Iran's military declared readiness to close Hormuz 'indefinitely' if Trump targeted power plants. The net result: a 4.86% selloff on extreme uncertainty, not fundamental supply change.
column12: Reuters: "Oil prices swung between gains and losses as investors weighed rising U.S. and Iranian threats over energy facilities against prospect of talks."

column2: Brent climbs past $113 on persistent Hormuz closure and Iran's threat to close strait indefinitely
column9: Pentagon deploying additional Marines; Trump rejected ceasefire; Bessent Iranian crude sanction-lift still pending
column10: Renewed escalation threat and lack of supply restoration drove fresh buying
column11: Brent climbed to $113.52 — up 61% from pre-conflict levels on 28 Feb — as Iran's military formally declared it would close the strait 'indefinitely' if Trump followed through on threats to bomb Iranian power plants. The Pentagon confirmed deployment of additional Marines (total ~6,000 regional) as Trump maintained aggressive posture. No tanker convoy had been successfully organised by any Western navy. The 140 mb Iranian sanction-lift floated by Bessent remained unexecuted. Markets were pricing in a scenario where the Strait of Hormuz remains at <10% capacity through April, consistent with Goldman Sachs' base case of $98–$110/bbl March–April average.
column12: Trading Economics: "Brent rose to 113.52 on March 23, up 60.40% over the past month."
